law_text: "On July 1, 1989, apparently unbeknownst to all the persons involved, the amendment of the Statute of April 27, 1989 on environmental protection, came into force."

law_text: Decree-law 1/30 (31 August 1992), which creates a free trade zone in Burundi; a series of ordinances from the Ministry of Commerce and Industry (750/415, 29 September 1992; 750/184, 29 May 1995), which implement the decree-law and exclude the claimant from this free trade zone

other_text: Settled. The Tribunal gave Burundi a choice: either compensate the claimant or reverse the contested measure. Burundi chose to compensate the claimant and signed a 10-year agreement granting the claimant tax benefits.

parties_drop: Compañía de Aguas del Aconquija S.A. and Vivendi Universal S.A. (formerly Compañía de Aguas del Aconquija, S.A. and Compagnie Générale des Eaux) v. Argentine Republic (I)
annul_status: partially annulled, then upheld in favor of investor upon resubmission, then upheld in annulment again
claimchoice_text:
"Here it is clear that, given the nature of the dispute between Claimants and the 
Province of Tucumán, it is not possible for this Tribunal to determine which 
actions of the Province were taken in exercise of its sovereign authority and 
which in the exercise of its rights as a party to the Concession Contract 
considering, in particular, that much of the evidence presented in this case has 
involved detailed issues of performance and rates under the Concession 
Contract.  To make such determinations, the Tribunal would have to undertake 
a detailed interpretation and application of the Concession Contract, a task left 
by the parties to that contract to the exclusive jurisdiction of the administrative 
courts of Tucumán."
law_text:
"In the case of 
ERSACT, which served as the regulatory body supervising the services 
rendered by CGE, Claimants point to two resolutions, Nos. 170 and 212, that 
“forced a reduction in the tariff and thereby created uncertainty as to what 
invoices had to be paid.";"The third act also harkens back to the first 
category and refers also to the fourth category (discussed below) because it 
concerns the changes by the Tucumán legislature in Law 6826 to the tariff 
schedule in the second renegotiated agreement"
lawchange_text: From the Tucumán gov. website: "Deroga [repeal] la Ley N° 6826 (Incorporación de disposiciones complementarias al Marco Regulatorio sobre la Titularidad y Prestación del Servicio de Agua Potable y Desagües Cloacales, modificando la Ley N° 6529 y sus modificatorias). Restablece la plena vigencia de las Leyes N° 6445 y 6529 y sus modificatorias."
other_text: The original tribunal decided not to rule on Vivendi's substantive claims on the grounds that Vivendi should have first sought recourse in domestic courts. Vivendi then sought annulment of the ruling on the grounds that international arbitration was indeed appropriate for their claims, which the annulment committee upheld. However, the annulment decision did not result in rulings on the claims themselves: this was relegated to the resubmission of the case (Vivendi v. Argentina II).

dissent_description: The dissenting member disagreed with the majority's conclusion that, under article 1121 of NAFTA, the tribunal lacked jurisdiction over this case as it was simultaneously being heard in domestic courts. He argues that article 1121 is meant to be a tool for claimants, not a restriction on them.
claimchoice_text:
"On  weighing  up  all  that  has  been  set  forth  hereinabove,  the
documentary exhibits and pleadings drawn up by the parties, this
Arbitral Tribunal is compelled to hold that it lacks jurisdiction to
judge the issue in dispute now brought before it, owing to breach
by  the  Claimant  of  one  of  the  requisites  laid  down  by  NAFTA
Article 1121(2)(b) and deemed essential in order to proceed with
submission of a claim to arbitration, namely, waiver of the
right to initiate  or  continue  before  any  tribunal  or  court,  dispute  settle-
ment  proceedings  with  respect  to  the  measures  taken  by  the
Respondent that are allegedly in breach of the NAFTA, the afore-
said  being  in  overall  accordance  with  the  provisions  of  said  legal
text and the ICSID Additional Facility."

law_text: "On May 29th, 1996 Canada and the U.S. entered into a bilateral agreement, the Softwood Lumber Agreement (SLA). lition for Fair Lumber Imports filed a

umping petition against Canadian softwood 
lumber. " Source: https://pennstatelaw.psu.edu/_file/aglaw/Lumber_Trade.pdf
lawcount_text: Part of the NAFTA arbitration saga of the US-Canada Softwood Lumber Dispute (see also: Pope and Talbot v. Canada
other_text: The softwood lumber dispute has been the longest-running U.S.-Canada trade dispute, and the Trump admin's recent imposition of tariffs on Canadian lumber is the most recent chapter. See: http://thehill.com/policy/finance/367221-canada-starts-appeal-of-us-softwood-lumber-tariff-decision

law_text:
" Since December 1, 1997, the IEPS law has been amended to bar rebates to 
cigarette resellers such as CEMSA, limiting such rebates to the “first sale” in Mexico.  Articles 
11 and 19 of the IEPS were amended so as to provide that tax rebates are not allowed on sales 
subsequent to those made to the retailer"

dissent_description: The respondent's appointed arbitrator, Jaroslav Hándl, wrote a scathing indictment of the way that the entire case was handled. First, he argued that he had been excluded from deliberation, and that the other arbitrators had been heavily biased against the Czech Republic. Then, he argued that the tribunal should have dismissed the case on jurisdiction. Finally, he argued for the dismissal of each claim brought forth by CME.
law_text: "Three years after the Media Council mandated the creation of and gave express approval to the NTS structure, it abruptly reversed its position, repudiated the arrangement it had officially approved, and forced NTS to surrender the exclusive right to use the Licence that CET 21 had contributed in return for its equity interest."
lawcount_text: Another UNCITRAL arbitration, Lauder v. Czech Republic, was initiated in response to the same actions of the Media Committee. While the tribunal in CME v. Czech Republic found the state in violation of all claims and awarded damages of $270m, the tribunal in Lauder v. Czech Republic found the state in violation of only one claim and awarded no damages to Lauder.

“The United States measures about which the Claimant complains in the present case comprise three tiers of legal provisions… The first tier consists of

other_text: While the events that lead to this case are fairly straightforward, the substance of the claim(s) made by the claimant are hard to decipher (so much so that UNCTAD classifies them as "unclear"). It seems to me that the main claim is indirect expropriation of intellectual property, based on the following submission from the claimant: "the State issued another public invitation to bid for  the  development  of  the  Soldado  Fields  utilising  the  same  process  and  utilising  information  and  know-how  derived  from  FWO, thereby seeking to expropriate the rights of FWO."

Long Title: Crompton (Chemtura) Corp. v. Government of Canada
Details of investment: Agricultural pesticide manufacturing company.

Economic Subsector: 20 - Manufacture of chemicals and chemical products
Arbitration Rules: UNCITRAL
Administering institution: PCA (Permanent Court of Arbitration)
Alleged Breaches: Indirect expropriation/Fair and equitable treatment/Minimum standard of treatment, including denial of justice claims/Most-favoured nation treatment

parties: Crompton Corp. (Chemtura) v. Canada
invst_type: Agricultural pesticide manufacturing company.
invst_dispute: Claims arising out of Canada’s Pest Management Regulatory Agency (PMRA) banning of the agro-chemical lindane on the basis of the chemical’s health and environmental effects.
parties_drop: NA
law_text: From the Notice of Arbitration: "On December 19, 2011, after having already terminated the use of lindane as a seed treatment for canola, Canada, through the PMRA (Pest Management Regulatory Agency) announced it would terminate all remaining lindane uses either through "phase out by suspension of registrations or voluntary discontinuation". / 20. On February 21, 2002, the PMRA terminated, through suspension, Crompton's remaining lindane registrations for: Reg. No 11422, Reg No. 14115 and Reg. No 15537./ 21. The PMRA terminated these lindane registrations wtihout the right to phase-out use notwithstanding the fact that Crompton had provided the sales and inventory information requested by the PMRA in order to be granted the right to phase-out use". From IA Reporter: "Chemtura says that the PMRA decision was not backed by a rigorous scientific risk assessment of lindane."
lawchange_text: no change
lawcount_text: Case broadly relates to cm71 - both dispute regulations that ban chemicals, although referring to different regulations.
other_text: The claimant just asks for compensation for damages; no attempts to ask for the reversal of the cancellation of its registrations.

law_text:
"The Claimants also allege that Article 8(1) of the Electricity Market Law No. 4268, enacted on February 20, 2001, eliminated the Treasury guarantees and that the law further restricted energy sales agreements with TEAS to terms not exceeding one year, as opposed to the previous practice of being in effect

enforce_type: judicial review by Sweden national courts, upheld
claimchoice_text: "Indeed, Article 8(1) only states that disputes under Articles 2(3), 4, 5 and 6 may be submitted to arbitration and there is nothing in the text to indicate that the arbitration may also include other questions arising under the Treaty. The Arbitral Tribunal therefore concludes that Mr Nagel's claims under Articles 2(2), 3(1) and 3(2) are not admissible in the present arbitration and must be rejected" \\"For the reasons indicated above, the Arbitral Tribunal concludes that Mr Nagel's rights under the Cooperation Agreement - alone or in conjunction with surrounding factors, such as the conduct of persons acting on behalf of the Government - did not constitute an asset and an investment protected under Article 1 of the Investment Treaty... It follows that Mr Nagel's claims based on Article 2(3) and Article 5(1) of the treaty must be dismissed."
law_text: "After 10 August 1994, when the Czech Government issued Resolution No. 428, there were discussions between Mr Nagel, Millicom and Cra in order to find a solution to the problems caused by the Government's new policy. However no agreement was reached."

law_text: "Not long after the second agreement was reached, a judicial injunction was requested by the Argentine Ombudsman ("Defensor del Pueblo de la Nación") against Decree 669/00 on the ground that it was both unconstitutional and contrary to Argentine law. other_text: Like Enron v. Argentina, this award was annulled in its entirety due to the Tribunal's failure to apply Article XI in their deliberation.

law_text:
From Award: "The Claimant submits that Poland deprived it of all three expectations. With the  enactment of the 2001 Sugar Law, the Respondent deprived Cargill of its expectation that no quota would be imposed prior to Poland's accession to the European Union. By setting a restrictive first quota, the Respondent frustrated Cargill's expectation that the quota would be set at a level accommodating Cargill's full capacity. And in its accession negotiations, the Respondent failed to request from the EU a quota level 
high enough to reflect Cargill's capacity."
lawchange_text: "The very last agricultural quota system in place, managing sugar production in the European Union, will be scrapped on 30 September 2017, after nearly 50 years. The decision to end the sugar quotas now was agreed between the European Parliament and Member States in the 2013 reform of the Common Agricultural policy (CAP) after a major reform and restructuring process initiated in 2006." source: http://europa.eu/rapid/press-release_IP-17-3487_en.htm

dissent_description: Interestingly, both party-appointed arbitrators filed separate opinions. The claimant-appointed arbitrator argued that the majority was too flexible in their application of the legitimate expectations test, and that a more objective approach would have rightly resulted in the finding of more FET violations. The respondent's appointee argued that the legitimate expectations test fundamentally misinterprets the intention of the FET provision, and that investors' "expectations" are not protected under FET.
law_text:
"According   to   Total,   the   Measures   enacted   by   Argentina   that   breached   its   
commitments  towards  Total  are  all  contained  in  or  took  place  pursuant  to  the  
Emergency Law of January 6, 2002. "
lawchange_text: "This month Mr Macri allowed the expiration of a 16-year-old economic-emergency law, which gave the president special powers over debt, taxes and the exchange rate" source: https://www.economist.com/the-americas/2018/01/18/argentinas-gamble-on-economic-gradualism-is-working-so-far -- "Eduardo Duhalde firma decreto Emergencia Económica que puso en vigencia la ley desde el 6 de enero de 2002" source: https://www.clarin.com/economia/gobierno-anuncio-final-ley-emergencia-economica_0_Sydj0DCmG.html
lawcount_text: Yet another case in which an investor filed arbitration against Argentina as a result of its economic emergency law.

dissent_description: The dissenting opinion does not refer to a specific claim, but rather to what the arbitration calls "an essential feature of investor-State arbitration". From the separate opinion of Andreas F. Lowenfeld: "(...) I believe the majority's discussion of the "Countermeasures Defence" clouds the essential feature of investor-State arbitration, as it has developed since the ICSID Convention of 1965, the very large number of Bilateral Investment Treaties, and Chapter Eleven of the NAFTA. The essence of each of these arrangements is that controversies between foreign investors and host states are insulated from political and diplomatic relations between states." (...) "I believe the majority goes to elaborate lenghts to refute and unprecedented and unjustified argument made on behalf of Mexico. Though it comes out right (para. 174, 181), the opinion loses sight of and distorts the essence of investor-State arbitration under NAFTA, ICSID and a mass of Bilateral Investment Treaties and Free Trade Agreements."
law_text: From the Notice of Arbitration: "23. This case represents a claim of precisely the type Chapter 11 was intended to redress. The claim, described more fully in the paragraphs that follow, arises out of the Government of Mexico's imposition, on January 1, 2002, of the HFCS Tax. This Tax, the culmination of a series of discriminatory measures taken by the Government of Mexico against the HFCS industry since it invested in Mexico post-NAFTA, was adopted to shiled the Mexican sugar industry from open and free market competition with the foreign-owned HFCS industry for Mexico's lucrative soft drink sweetener market. By imposing the Tax on soft drinks containing HFCS but excluding from its scope soft drinks that use sugar as their sweetening ingredient, Mexico purposefully eliminated the most important market for HFCS production in Mexico and, in a single stroke, handed the entire soft drink sweetener market to Mexico's domestic sugar producer(...)"
lawchange_text: This issue has also been disputed in the WTO. The WTO ruled in favor of the U.S., and there is some evidence that the government revoked that law in January of 2007, following the ruling and not necessarily the investment dispute (link: https://bit.ly/2yLC2Dp). In 2014, Mexico passed a new law that applied taxes on all sugary drinks, regardless of what the sugar is made of.
lawcount_text: ct205 and ct245 dispute the exact same law. / Slightly related to ct191. It does not refer to exactly the same law and the respondent is different, but both law broadly refer to imposing taxes/quotas on specific types of sugar.
other_text: Several documents pertaining this case were available, but not the Award. Therefore, it is not possible to know whether the claims of Indirect Expropriation and Other (Performance Requirements) were ruled on or not.

law_text: From Award: "On December 30,2001, with effect from January 1,2002, the Mexican Congress amended Articles 1,2,3 and 8 of the Ley del Impuesto Especial sobre Producci6n y Servicios, (the "[BPS Amendment" hereinafter) imposing a 20 percent excise tax on soft drinks and syrups and the same tax on services used to transfer and distribute soft drinks and syrups. This tax only applied to soft drinks and syrups that used any sweetener other than cane sugar, such as high fructose corn syrup (HFCS). Soft drinks and syrups sweetened exclusively with cane sugar were taxexempt. The tax was repealed as of January 1, 2007. For purposes of the present Award, the excise tax measures affecting 'soft drinks and sugar resulting from the IEPS Amendment will hereinafter be referred to as "the Tax."
lawchange_text: This issue has also been disputed in the WTO. The WTO ruled in favor of the U.S., and there is some evidence that the government revoked that law in January of 2007, following the ruling and not necessarily the investment dispute (link: https://bit.ly/2yLC2Dp). In 2014, Mexico passed a new law that applied taxes on all sugary drinks, regardless of what the sugar is made of.

other_text:
The case contains a "Decision on the Requests for Correction, Supplementary Decision and Interpretation." The tribunal's decision on these requetsts is: "
For the foregoing reasons, this Arbitral Tribunal hereby: 1. Dismisses the Claimants' requests to supplement the Award; 2. Allows the Respondent's request for the correction of the Award, and orders by way of correction pursuant to Article 56 AF Rules that in subparagraphs 4 and 5 of the dispositive part of the Award (paragraph 304) the word "Claimants" shall be deleted and replaced by the name "ALMEX". 3. Dismisses the Claimants' request for the interpretation of the Award; 4. Dismisses the Respondent's request in respect of redaction and disclosure of the Award. 5. Orders that the Claimants shall pay the fees and expenses of the members of the Arbitral Tribunal and the expenses and charges of the Secretariat in relation to these post-Award proceedings, and shall pay to the Respondent the sum of US$54,753.00 (FIFTY-FOUR THOUSAND SEVEN HUNDRED AND FIFTY THREE UNITED STATES DOLLARS) in respect of its legal costs. "

dissent_description: In a separate opinion, one arbitrator agrees with all of the rulings on jurisdiction/merits but offers some alternative legal reasoning to the majority.
claimchoice_text:
"In  light  of  the  Tribunal’s  conclusion  that,  by  prematurely  revoking  the  EGO  24
incentives  in  the  manner  that  it  did,  the  Respondent  breached  its  
obligation  to  treat  
the Claimants’ investments fairly and equitably, the Tribunal does not need to address 
the Claimants’ remaining claims."
law_text:
"In the latest formulation of their case, the Claimants argue that Romania entered into 
these binding commitments through EGO 24; its implementing legislation, in particular 
GD 194/1999; and the issuance of Permanent Investor Certificates (PICs) to the three 
Corporate  Claimants... The Claimants contend that Romania’s revocation of 
these incentives effective 22 February 2005 (approximately 4 years before they were 
set to expire) breached that commitment or undermined that legitimate expectation."

parties_drop: NA
claimchoice_text:
"This  Tribunal  does  not  have  jurisdiction  to  consider  claims  under  
NAFTA  Article  1116  for  an  alle
ged  breach  of  NAFTA  Article  
1102(1) where all of the Claimants’ i
nvestments at issue are located in 
the Canadian portion of the North American Free Trade Area and the 
Claimants do not seek to make, are not making and have not made any 
investments in the territory of the United States of America. "
law_text:
"From  May  20,  2003  to  July
  14,  2005,  the  United  States  
imposed   and   maintained   an   absolute   prohibition   on   the   
shipment of live ca
ttle from Canada under 
the authority of the 
Animal  Health  Protection  Act  
(AHPA).  This  ban  was  imposed  
in  spite  of  abundant  evidence  that  Canadian-origin  livestock  
and beef products were safe and posed minimal risk of BSE to 
the United States."
lawchange_text: "In March 2005, the United States completed a rule-making process to provide the necessary authority to reopen the border to certain classes of live ruminants and a broader range of ruminant products. This so-called U.S. “BSE minimal-risk rule” amends the requirements regulating the importation of animals and animal products, and creates a new category for regions in which BSE has been detected in the national herd but in which precautionary measures have been taken that reduce the risk of BSE being exported to the United States. The rule, which was scheduled to take effect on 7 March 2005, adds Canada to this new category." source: https://lop.parl.ca/content/lop/ResearchPublications/prb0301-e.htm

dissent_description: One arbitrator argues that the majority has misinterpreted the MFN clause as it pertains to the application of provisions from other BITS; he thus disagrees with the decision to throw the case out on jurisdiction.
claimchoice_text:
"The  Claimant  does  not  yet  have  standing  to  assert  its 
claim   s  under  the  German-Argentine  BIT,  because it  has  not  yet  satisfied 
the Treaty’s  Article  10  conditions precedent to invoke international arbitration.
 As such, the Tribunal lacks jurisdiction at present to entertain the Claimant’s MFN or any other claim"
law_text:
"In  particular,  the  Claimant  alleges  that  the  following  measures,  as  applied  to  its 
investment, violated its rights as a foreign investor under the German
-Argentine BIT: Law 25,561 of January 2002 (“the Emergency Law”)… Executive Decree 214/2002... Laws 25,563 and 25,589"
lawchange_text: "This month Mr Macri allowed the expiration of a 16-year-old economic-emergency law, which gave the president special powers over debt, taxes and the exchange rate" source: https://www.economist.com/the-americas/2018/01/18/argentinas-gamble-on-economic-gradualism-is-working-so-far -- "Eduardo Duhalde firma decreto Emergencia Económica que puso en vigencia la ley desde el 6 de enero de 2002" source: https://www.clarin.com/economia/gobierno-anuncio-final-ley-emergencia-economica_0_Sydj0DCmG.html
lawcount_text: Yet another case in which an investor filed arbitration against Argentina as a result of its economic emergency law.

law_text: From Award: "Cargill argues that Mexico's imposition of a tax on soft drinks containing HFCS and its failure to issue import permits violated NAFTA Articles 1102, 1103, 1105, 1106 and 1110, and resulted in damages to Cargill's investment." The issue in dispute is that only soft drinks containing HFCS and beet sugar were subjected to taxation, while those made with sugar cane were exempt.
lawchange_text: This issue has also been disputed in the WTO. The WTO ruled in favor of the U.S., and there is some evidence that the government revoked that law in January of 2007, following the ruling and not necessarily the investment dispute (link: https://bit.ly/2yLC2Dp). In 2014, Mexico passed a new law that applied taxes on all sugary drinks, regardless of what the sugar is made of.
lawcount_text: ct204 and ct205 dispute the exact same law. / Slightly related to ct191. It does not refer to exactly the same law and the respondent is different, but both law broadly refer to imposing taxes/quotas on specific types of sugar. /
other_text: Respondent opened judicial review by national courts to dispute jurisdiction.

law_text: "On 30 July 1998, Slovenia disconnected electricity lines from the Krško NPP to Croatia, terminated all electricity deliveries to HELp, and issued a Governmental "Decree" which HEP claimed affected its rights as a 50 percent owner and manager of the plant."
lawchange_text: "Slovenia did not ratify the 2001 Agreement until late February of 2003, and it did not resume deliveries of electricity from the Krško NPP to HEP until 19 April 2003"
lawsettle_text: "Following Slovenia's displacement of HEP from its role as a 50 percent owner of the Krško NPP, the Governments of the two countries entered into negotiations aimed at restoring HEP's rights… It was agreed that 30 June 2002 would be the date of resumption of deliveries of electricity to HELP, and the date up to which all financial claims were to be waived."
other_text: This was a strange case. The 2009 "Decision on the Treaty Interpretation Issue" document stated that HEP had no standing to bring claims against Slovenia under the ECT, but that their claims under a sui generis agreement (a one-off, "unique" agreement) were admissable and to be decided in a later ruling. This was recorded by UNCTAD as a victory for the respondent, when in reality it was not a final ruling at all. Ultimately, the final award found that Slovenia had breached the terms of the sui generis agreement, and was ordered to pay 20m EUR plus interest in damages. However, no specific violations are mentioned by name under the sui generis agreement.

claimchoice_text: From Award: "98. The Tribunal observes that the conditions enumerated in Article 6 are cumulative. In other terms, if any of those conditions is violated, there is a breach of Article 6. The Tribunal will first examine whether or not subparagraph (c) relating to the provisions of a just compensation has been breached. If it arrives to the conclusion that it has, it will not be necessary for it to consider whether, as alleged by the Claimants, the other conditions provided for in that Article or the provisions of Article 3 have also been breached. / 107. As a consequence, the Tribunal concludes that Zimbabwe breached its obligation under Article 6(c) of the BIT to pay just compensation to the Claimants. Accordingly, as stated in paragraph 98 above, the Tribunal does not need to consider whether other provisions of the BIT have been violated."
law_text: From Award: "34. Finally, on 14 September 2005, the Zimbabwe Constitution was again amended. In implementation of the amended Section 16A of the new text, all the agricultural land owned by the Claimants were “acquired by and vested in the State with full title therein . . . .”16 In fact however, the Claimants had all effectively been deprived of their properties at a much earlier date, either by Section 8 orders or through the invasion of their farms by war veterans and settlers. / 22. Under Section 8 of the Act, the Acquiring Authority is authorized to issue an acquisition of land order which has the immediate effect of divesting the owner of its interests in the property." Note: "the Act"is the Land Acquisition Act authorized by the Government of Zimbabwe in 1992.
lawchange_text: "It comes in the wake of the gazetting yesterday, following Presidential assent, of the Land (Consequential Provisions) Act that forbids using or occupying gazetted land without lawful authority… The Act repeals the Rural Land Occupiers (Protection from Eviction) Act. " source: http://link.galegroup.com/apps/doc/A153971448/OVIC?u=txshracd2598&sid=OVIC&xid=3c67fcf1
lawcount_text: cm102 disputes the same law
lawafter_text: The government of President Emmerson Mnangagwa has ordered an end to illegal land takeovers, by putting takeovers on hold, but not changing the amendment. Local news source: http://www.cfuzim.org/~cfuzimb/index.php/newspaper-articles-2/land-issues/3294-zimbabwe-stops-farm-acquisitions

claimchoice_text: The Claimant argues that if the Tribunal "make[s] a finding of unlawful expropriation, with the natural damages consequences", it need not consider the other claims (full protection and security and fair and equitable treatment). The reasoning is that once compensation is determined for expropriation, "there can be, virtually by definition, no loss or damage left to be compensated separately based on a breach of other, lesser standards." Having found that Venezuela is liable for unlawful expropriation, the Tribunal does not rule on other claims.
law_text: Decree-Law 1546 (Tierras y Desarrollo Agrario, 13 November 2001), cited in the filings as the "2001 Land Law"
lawchange_text: There was no change in the Land Law itself, but there was a change in its interpretation. In 2013, the Venezuelan Supreme Court passed a binding interpretation of articles 175, 228 and 229, which envision the possibility of appeals to land expropriation. See index of Venezuelan laws: http://www.indiley.com/Indice/ILD-Junio-18.pdf (p. 89-90)
other_text: Venezuela applied to annul the award, but the Tribunal decided to let the enforcement proceed: http://tinyurl.com/ya5tpusg

other_text: It should be stated that the executive order was only one of many offending actions alleged by the claimant; most had to do with off-the-books harrassment by police and regulatory agencies, as well as national courts. It is also noteworthy that this tribunal found that the FET standard (as well as protection + security and discriminatory treatment) was breached as the result of many individual offenses which, on their own, would not have constituted a breach. However, the tribunal was not convinced that the Claimant deserved pecuniary damages as a result of these breaches.

law_text:
"On  February  20,  2001,  a  new  Electricity  Market  Law  (“Law  4628”)  was  enacted,  
consistent with the then-existing EU Directive 96/92 (Exh. C-13). The entire transmission 
network   would   be   operated   by   a   State-owned   company:   the   Turkish   Electricity   
Transmission   Joint   Stock   Company   (“TEIAS”)   (Exh.   R-94).   Vertically   integrated   
companies,   including   CEAS   and   Kepez,   would   no   longer   be   able   to   engage   in

lawchange_text: "Law No. 4628 was amended with Electricity Market Law No. 6446 (EML), which entered into force on 30 March 2013. Law No. 4628 is still in force, but its name has changed to the Law on the Organisation and Duties of the Energy Market Regulatory Authority. Therefore, Law No. 4628 only regulates the duties and rights of EMRA, while the EML regulates market activities." sources: http://boden-law.com/publication/electricity-regulation-2017/                   https://www.ecolex.org/details/legislation/electricity-market-law-no-6446-lex-faoc150285/?
lawcount_text: Related to Electricity Market Law 4628
other_text: This case is notable in that the Tribunal rules that the investor's claims were fraudulent, malicious, and constitute an abuse of process. In addition to bearing the full cost of the arbitration, the claimant was ordered to pay Turkey's ICSID dues ($325,000).

claimchoice_text:
From the Partial Award on the Merits: "4. In view of the Tribunal's decision in section 2 above regarding the breach of Article II(7) of the BIT, and given that the relief sought b Notey Claimants with respect to its additional claims does not go beyond that sought pursuant to the claim regarding Article II(7), the Tribunal need not decide the Claimants' claims regarding other breaches of the BIT or customary international law". Note: Article II(7) of the BIT refers to the claim of "failure to provide effective means of asserting claims and enforcing rights", as established by the treaty. / "38. In the alternative, the Claimants argue that the Ecuadorian courts’ recent decisions are manifestly unjust, grossly incompetent and constitute a further independent denial of justice
under customary international law. Direct proof of bad faith by the courts is not necessary in this regard: a denial of justice can be found from a clear and malicious misapplication of the law."
law_text: From Partial Award: "35. In the first place, the Claimants submit that through the Ecuadorian courts’ 15-year delay and refusal to render a judgment in TexPet’s seven cases, Ecuador has patently violated its own laws and has committed a denial of justice under international law. The courts’ undue delays and refusals are in clear violation of Ecuador’s own laws governing judicial proceedings, including Ecuador’s 1998 Constitution, Organic Law on the Judiciary, and Code of Civil Procedure, as well as the American Convention on Human Rights"
lawchange_text: no evidence - the government of Ecuador does not keep records before 2008 online. It was not possible to verify whether the seven lawsuits Chevron filed against the government in national courts were ruled on after the arbitral award was published. The practices disputed by the investor do not constitute a single law or policy, but rather, judicial misconduct. It is not clear whether these lawsuits led the government to reform or change its judicial system, but  has withdrawn from the ICSID convention to avoid new litigation and it looks like the Chevron lawsuits played a role in it this process. " On 12 June 2009, Ecuador's legislature voted to denounce the Convention, and on 2 July, President Rafael Correa signed Executive Order No. 1823, the final domestic act required for denunciation of the Convention." link: https://tmsnrt.rs/2uyWJg
lawcount_text: Relates to cm67.
other_text: This is a very convoluted case and might need somebody else to tak a look at it. Ecuador started post-arbitral measures. First, it requested to the District Court of the Hague that the Interim Award, the Partial Award and the Final Award be set aside. Ecuador argued lack of a valid arbitration agreement and breach of the mandate and unreasoned arbitral awards. The awards were upheld by the court. Then, Ecuador challenged the final Award in DC Court's, which was also rejected. Finally, the goverment sought to challenge the Final Award at the Dutch Supreme Court, but the original decision was also upheld in this forum.

dissent_description: One arbitrator argues that  Venezuela acted in good faith and the expropriation is lawful. In his view, the Tribunal should ask "whether the expropriating State provided for compensation, and if it did, to ascertain that what was offered was not 'illusory' amounting to a refusal to pay compensation." For him, the answer to both questions is yes.
claimchoice_text: jurisdiction
law_text: The Tribunal discusses whether Article 22 of Decree-Law 356 (Ley de Promoción y Protección de Inversiones, 3 October 1999) gives consent to international arbitration under ICSID, concluding that it does.
lawchange_text: Decree-Law 356 was replaced by Decree-Law 1438 (Ley de Inversiones Extranjeras, 18 November 2014), which itself was again replaced by a constitutional law (Ley Constitucional de Inversión Extranjera Productiva, 29 December 2017). See index of Venezuelan laws: http://www.indiley.com/Indice/ILD-Junio-18.pdf (p. 58)
lawcount_text: Parallel case with Mobil and Others v. Venezuela (ct304)
other_text: This is a convoluted case with multiple changes in the composition of the Tribunal and various proposals for disqualification, but the Tribunal's interim decision upheld the initial decision on jurisdiction and merits.

claimchoice_text:
Arbitrary treatment : "Consequently, the Tribunal considers that, once 
a breach of the obligations in the first sentence  has  been  found,  a  further  examination  based  on  the  second  sentence  is  not  required." Protection and Security: "the Arbitral Tribunal 
considers  that  where,  as  in  the  present  case,  there  has  been  a  failure  to  give  an  
investment  fair  and  equitable  treatment,  it  is  not  necessary  to  examine  whether  there  
has also been a failure to ensure full protection and security."  Umbrella clause: "As  regards  the  allegation  under  (c),  the  Arbitral  Tribunal,  with  reference  to  its  views  on  the  distinction  between  contractual  claims  and  treaty  claims,  finds  that  the  obligations  referred  to  are  contractual  obligations  between  AGBA  and  the  Province  
which fall outside the Tribunal’s jurisdiction (see paras. 183-185 above)."
law_text:
"First,  the  Provincial  Emergency  Law  brought  any  potential  investment  and  
financing  in  AGBA  to  a  halt,  and  the  Concession  Contract  was  literally  finished.  
Second, the Province’s mandated renegotiation process was a failure." "Finally,  on  July  11,  2006,  the  Province  Governor,  by  Decree  No.  1666/06,  
terminated the Concession Agreement due to AGBA’s fault pursuant to Article 14.1.3 
(a),  (b),  (h),  (i)  and  (k)  of  the  Concession  Contract.  By  Decree  No.  1677/06,  the  
Province Governor, on July 13, 2006, transferred AGBA’s water and sewage service 
concession to ABSA. "

"On  24  January  2006,  the  Court  of  Appeal  issued  a  judgment  annulling  the  Final  Award  
principally  on  the  basis  that  the  FIDIC  Tribunal  had  made  an  error  in  concluding  that  
Article 789 of the Civil Code, upon which the FIDIC Tribunal had based its Final Award, 
was  applicable  in  the  circumstances." " The  Court  of  Cassation  therefore  dismissed  ATA’s  recourse  (and,  in  doing  so,  also  dismissed  APC’s  recourse  in  this  regard),  by  declaring  that  the  Court  of  Appeal’s  findings as regards the finality of the Final Award were not, in any event, dispositive and 
had no effect on its ultimate decision."
other_text: Notably, the tribunal ordered that Jordan cease all domestic court proceedings against the investor in addition to awarding pecuniary damages.

bona fide
 investment, as it was performed

law_text:
"In February 2000, following the adoption of a law providing for a conversion process 
for certain administrative law concession contracts (Law No. 4501), the First Project Company sought to 
convert the Concession Contract to a private law contract. About a year later, another new law
in Turkey eliminated Treasury Guarantees for certain energy sectorprojects not finalized before December 31, 2002 
(Law  No.  4628) and  made  certain  restrictions  to  energy  sales  agreements. Claimant asserted that the 
Respondent’s actions in connection with the conversion process and some of the legislative changes 
led to the loss of its investment and violated a number of investment protection provisions of the ECT
and the BIT."
lawchange_text: "Law No. 4628 was amended with Electricity Market Law No. 6446 (EML), which entered into force on 30 March 2013. Law No. 4628 is still in force, but its name has changed to the Law on the Organisation and Duties of the Energy Market Regulatory Authority. Therefore, Law No. 4628 only regulates the duties and rights of EMRA, while the EML regulates market activities." sources: http://boden-law.com/publication/electricity-regulation-2017/                   https://www.ecolex.org/details/legislation/electricity-market-law-no-6446-lex-faoc150285/?

parties_drop: Achmea B.V. v. Slovak Republic (formerly Eureko BV v Slovak Republic) (I)
enforce_type: judicial review by natl courts, Germany upheld 2012, 2014, 2016
claimchoice_text:
From Award: "284. Claimant argued that there was also a violation of Article 3(2) of the Treaty, which requires each Contracting Party to accord “full protection and security” to the investment. Where, as here, the complaint is essentially that the investment was not
protected against government policies, the question whether there has been a breach of the Treaty is inseparable from the question whether the policies in question were fair and equitable. The Tribunal sees no need in the circumstances of this case to consider
the claim under Article 3(2) separately from the claim under Article 3(1). It regards its decision in respect of the claim under Article 3(1) as disposing of both claims/ 286. The Tribunal finds that the ban on profits was inconsistent with Respondent’s obligations under this Article. In principle, any losses arising from the application of that ban to Claimant would be recoverable in damages. In the present case, however, the facts are such that the violation and the injury arising from the temporary adoption of the ban on profits are subsumed within the violation and the injury arising from the breach of the ‘fair and equitable treatment’ obligation. The Tribunal accordingly records that Respondent was in breach of Article 4, but it is not necessary to consider the question of losses arising from that breach any further"
law_text:
From Award: "Claimant complains that various legislative measures introduced by Respondent after a change in government in July 2006 constituted a systematic reversal of the 2004 liberalisation of the Slovak health insurance market that had prompted Eureko to
invest in the Slovak Republic’s health insurance sector. The "2007 Reforms" are comprised by: amended network requirement (Act No. 578/2004 Coll. by Act No. 653/2007 Coll. and amendment of Decree 751/2004 Coll. by Decree 504/2007 Coll. and adoption of Decree No. 640/2008 Coll.); ban on brokers (amendment of sections 6(15) and 6(16) of Health Insurance Companies Act by Act No. 12/2007 Coll.); ban on profits (Amendment of Section 15(6) of Health Insurance Companies Act by Act No. 530/2007 Coll. and amendment of Section 86d of Health Insurance Companies Act by Act No. 594/2007 Coll.); ban on transfers (Amendment of Section 61 of Health Insurance Companies Act by Act No. 192/2009 Coll.); cap on operating expenses (Act No. 522/2006); repositioning of the Regulator (Amendment of Section 22 of Health Insurance Companies Act by Act No. 12/2007 Coll.)."
lawchange_text: no change
lawcount_text: RD 530/2007 and 594/2007 are also disputed on cm83. This case also is broadly related to cm44 and cm245, but it does not dispute the same specific laws.
lawafter_text: Slovakia appealed the 2010 jurisdictional decision to the German Courts. The arbitral tribunal decision was upheld. Then, Slovakia appealed the 2012 award decision to German courts, employing the same argument in the 2010 appeal that the BIT in question was incompatible with EU law. No specific claims were appealed, rather the law compatibility as a whole. The arbitral tribunal decisions were also upheld with regards to the award. Several EU Member States and the EU Commission have intervened in these proceedings: https://www.iareporter.com/articles/analysis-unpacking-the-reasoning-in-the-advocate-generals-opinion-on-the-achmea-v-slovakia-case/. Another appeal was brought to the ECJ, which decided to overturn the arbitral tribunal decision, by declaring intra-EU BITs incompatible with EU law. A final decision on whether the award will be dismissed or not is awaited. The law did not change.

law_text: From Award: "40. Prior to its accession to the European Union in 2004, and in anticipation of the EU's Pharmaceuticals Directive,3 Poland proceeded to enact legislation in accordance with its obligations under the Europe Agreement. Thus, on 6 September 2001, Poland adopted the Pharmaceutical Law and the Act on Introductory Provisions of the Pharmaceutical Law ("Act on Introductory Provisions"). The Pharmaceutical Law and the Act on Introductory Provisions entered into force on 1 October 2002, and together they represent the main sources of regulation of pharmaceutical products in Poland. / From IA Reporter: "As part of Poland’s EU accession, the country was obliged to harmonize its pharmaceutical laws with those of the European Union, and this included a process whereby thousands of medicines were reviewed by regulatory authorities. According to this source, at least one of Servier’s popular medicines – and possibly two – were screened out by this harmonization process, as a result of their not being more effective than generic alternatives"

claimchoice_text: "The claimant's letter of 11 September 2015, confirming that, pursuant to the terms of the Settlement Deed, the Claimant and the Respondent… have agreed that: the Respondent shall withdraw any and all of its objections to the Tribunal's jurisdiction and the merits of the Claimant's claims; the Arbitral Tribunal has jurisdiction under the Treaty to determine the quantum of the compensation to be awarded to the Claimant"
law_text:
"Also on 4 July 2011, the Ministry of Finance issued the 
Belize Telecommunication (Assumption of Control over Belize Telemedia Limited) Order, 2011

lawchange_text:
"On 11 June 2012, the Supreme Court of Belize granted the following orders: A declaration is granted that the Belize Telecommunications (Assumption of Control 
Over  Belize  Telemedia  Limited)  Order  2011,  No.  70  of  2011,  (the  2011  Order)  is unlawful, null and void"
other_text: Two things: first, this case was strange in that the parties reached a settlement on the merits but left the quantum up to the tribunal. Therefore, there exists a 140-page award for this case despite the lack of rulings on the jurisdiction or merits. Second, it's worth noting that while the offending executive order was nullified by the supreme court, its effect was not; Belize Telemedia remained under state ownership.

claimchoice_text: The tribunal declined jurisdiction because it found that the investor had failed to comply with a waiver requirement contained in the CAFTA when the company neglected to discontinue litigation in El Salvador's courts before turning to international arbitration (IA Reporter). In the Award, they note: "Given the Tribunal's decisions regarding the application of Article 37(2)(b) of the Mining Law and its dismissal of the Claimant's pleaded claim for damages as regards the El Dorado Project, it is unecessary here for the Tribunal to address further and decide separately the other issues arising under Article 37(2)(c) and Article 37(2)(d) of the Mining Law. It would unduly lengthen this already long Award and, whatever the answer, it could not affect the end-result of this Award. Hence, the Tribunal declines to do so. However, nothing should be assumed (one way or another) as to how the Tribunal would have decided these issues, if it had been required to do so."
law_text: From Award: "a de facto ban on metallic mining by the Respondent's President in March 2008, the Salvadoran Government illegitimately swept aside the legal and regulatory regime upon which the Claimant had relied, depriving it of the value of its significant investments in El Salvador"
lawchange_text: The ban was not reversed. From Lexis Nexis: "The two successive governments of the leftist Farabundo Mart'I National Liberation Front have maintained this de facto moratorium since 2009" . And, in April 2017: " El Salvador, Central America's smallest country, has become the first country in the world to pass a law banning metal mining in all its forms, setting a precedent for other nations in the world to follow, according to activists and local residents" . Link: https://bit.ly/2t0DL1o
lawcount_text: cm85 disputes the same "law" (revokation of permits and denial in issuing new permits, constituting a de facto ban on mining activities, per the claimants' argument)
other_text: This case includes an amicus curiae brief filed by 8 Salvadoran NGOs that contended that an expansion of mining activity could imperil El Salvador's water supply and environment (IA Reporter).

law_text: From Award: 46. In 2006, there was a general election in the Slovak Republic, as a result of which a new government came to power. On 24 November 2007, the National Council of the Slovak Republic adopted Act No. 530/2007 Coll. ("Amendment I"), which amended the 2004 law on health insurance companies. A second Amendment, Act No. 594/2007 Coll. ("Amendment II"), was adopted on 19 December 2007. Amendments I and II entered into force on 1 January 2008. These Amendments prohibited Slovak public health insurance providers from distributing as dividends profits derived from public health insurance and required them to reinvest all such profits in the provision of public health care.
lawchange_text: no change
lawcount_text: RD 530/2007 and 594/2007 are also disputed on cm49. This case also is broadly related to cm44 and cm245, but it does not dispute the same specific laws.

parties_drop: Commerce Group Corp. and San Sebastian Gold Mines, Inc. v. Republic of El Salvador
claimchoice_text: From Award: "140. FOR THE FOREGOING REASONS, the Tribunal renders the following decisions: (1) DETERMINES that the dispute is not within its jurisdiction and competence pursuant to CAFTA; ... (3) DISMISSES all other claims or requests for relief."
law_text: From Award: "On 13 September 2006, MARN (Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources) revoked the environmental permits of the San Sebastian Gold Mine and the San Cristóbal Plant and Mine, thereby effectively terminating Claimants' rights to mine and process gold and silver". (...) "In the interim, over the course of 2006 and 2007, Commerce/Sanseb applied to MARN for an environmental permit for the New San Sebastian Exploration License and the Nueva Esparta exploration license, and then to Respondent's Ministry fo Economy for the extension of exploration licenses. The requested environmental permits were not granted, and on 28 October 2008, El Salvador's Ministry of Economy denied Commerce/Sanseb's application citing Commerce/Sanseb/s failure to secure an environment permit". Note: the investor claims that the denial of permits represented a de facto ban on mining activities by the country.
lawchange_text: Permits were not granted after the case was concluded. On March 2017, the legislative approved on na de jure ban on all mining for gold and other metals in the country. Link: https://nyti.ms/2nO5Qbt
lawcount_text: cm82 disputes the same "law" (revokation of permits and/or denial in issuing new permits, constituting a de facto ban on mining activities, per the claimants' argument)
other_text: The investors tried to overturn the arbitral award that declined jurisdiction (issued on March 2011). Annulment was sought, but claimants struggled to pay advance, and then moved to find third-party funding for the case. Failure in guaranteeing the funds led to a decision of discontinuation of the case by ICSID on August 2013. From IA Reporter: https://bit.ly/2LcIxkc and https://bit.ly/2mdV0cP

law_text: The claimant disputed the 2005 Constitutional Amendment, section 16b, which had the purpose to redistribute land to black Zimbabweans: 117 The effect of Section 16B was to acquire and vest in the State title to every property in Zimbabwe in relation to which a Section 5 Notice had been issued on or before 8 July 2005, if the Notice was identified in Schedule 7 of the amended Constitution. The right that had previously existed to challenge the acquisition of land was removed. Section 16B(6) also criminalized the continued possession or occupation of land expropriated pursuant to the 2005 Constitutional Amendment.
lawchange_text:
"In 2012, a new Constitution was put to the Zimbabwean people in a referendum, and subsequently enacted  into  law  in  early  2013  (the  “2013  Constitution”)  (see   CLEX-331;  Tr.  Day 4,  pp.  1190-1191).  On the matter of agricultural land, addressed in Chapter 16 of the 2013 Constitution, the principles relating to compensation for land acquired by the Respondent were again changed, this time  to  provide  expressly  for  the  full  compensation  of  any  “indigenous  Zimbabwean”  whose  agricultural  land  was  acquired,  in  contradistinction  to  the  compensation  of  non-indigenous Zimbabweans.    The  2013  Constitution  also  reaffirmed  that  foreign  nationals  protected  by  a  BIT  whose agricultural land had been acquired are entitled to full compensation for that land pursuant 
to  the  terms  of  the  BIT"
lawcount_text: ct257 disputes the same law.
lawafter_text: The government of President Emmerson Mnangagwa has ordered an end to illegal land takeovers, by putting takeovers on hold, but not changing the amendment. Local news source: http://www.cfuzim.org/~cfuzimb/index.php/newspaper-articles-2/land-issues/3294-zimbabwe-stops-farm-acquisitions
other_text: Joint dispute with cm103

claimchoice_text:
Umbrella clause: "The  Tribunal  concludes  that  it  is  not  necessary  to  separately  decide  upon Claimants’ claim  that  the  ‘tariff  in  exchange  for  investment’  scheme  is  in  breach  of  the  Umbrella Clauses  under  Article  10(1)  of  the  ECT  and  Article  II(2)(c)  of  the  BIT." Failure to promote: "The  Arbitral  Tribunal  is  of  the  opinion  that  the  first  sentence  of  Article  10(1)  of  the ECT  does  not  establish  an  independent  standard  affording  protection  going  beyond  the protection  already  afforded  under  the  more  specific  protection  standards  set  out  in  the remaining  part  of  Article  10(1)  of  the  ECT.  Consequently,  no  independent  claim  may  be 
based on the first sentence of Article 10(1) of the ECT." Arbitrary treatment: "To the extent that such claim would be directed at the specific effects of the ‘tariff in exchange for investment’ policy, 
the way that Claimants have construed their claim appears no different from their claim for 
breach  of  the  FET  standard  and  is  therefore  covered  thereby,  so  that  it  is  unnecessary  to 
determine  the  existence  of  a  separate  breach  under  the  duty  to  refrain  from  adopting 
unreasonable or arbitrary measures. "
law_text:
"The  situation  was  then  further 
exacerbated  by  the  enactment  and  implementation  of  the  2012  Electricity  Law,  which Claimants  consider  to  be  an  arbitrary  and  irrational  piece  of  legislation,  which  did  not pursue  any  legitimate  policy  goals  and  which  was  clearly  aimed  at  preventing  Claimants from making any distributable return on or of their investment in their electricity generation 
activities in the EKO until 2016 at the earliest."

parties: Peter A. Allard v. The Government of Barbados
invst_type: Ownership of a wildlife sanctuary consisting of 34-acre natural wetlands.
invst_dispute: Claims arising out of alleged environmental damage and indirect expropriation by the Government of the Graeme Hall Nature Sanctuary, a wildlife sanctuary in Barbados owned by the claimant.
parties_drop: Peter A. Allard v. The Government of Barbados
law_text: From Award: 3. The Claimant claims that Barbados has “failed to take reasonable and necessary environmental protection measures and, through its organs and agents, has directly contributed to the contamination of the Claimant’s eco-tourism site, thereby destroying the value of his investment.” /43. " In 2005, a failure at the South Coast Sewage Treatment Plant, operated by the Barbados Water Authority, resulted in the emergency discharge of raw sewage into Graeme Hall Swamp. /44. In June 2007, Mr. Allard began trying to sell the Sanctuary. /45. On 15 April 2008, an amended Barbados National Physical Development Plan, first proposed in 2003, came into effect (" 2003 Plan" ). The 2003 Plan reclassified a zone located to the north of the Sanctuary that had been allocated to recreational and agricultural uses under the 1986 Plan into two zones: a "predominantly residential"  zone, closest to the Sanctuary, and an " urban corridor"  (the "Zoning Changes").
lawchange_text: There is no evidence that Barbados has taken measures to protect its mangroves.

claimchoice_text: The tribunal declined jurisdiction. According to IA Reporter: "As we speculated in our initial reporting about the case, questions about the inter-relationship of the 2009 BIT and the less-investor-friendly 1980s-era China-Belgium bilateral investment treaty were likely to arise in the case. The older treaty was in place during the time when Belgium’s key measures were taken. However, the claimants waited until 2012 to bring a claim under the newer BIT, after its entry into force. Notably, the newer treaty contains a provision that forecloses claims by investors that have taken prior steps to “judicialize” or “arbitrate” a dispute while the older treaty was still in place" Link: https://bit.ly/2ui9Ymo
law_text: From Award: "On September 28, 2008 Belgium agreed with the Netherlands and Luxembourg to carry out the following operations on the next day: (1) Belgium would acquire a 49.93% stake in FBB, via Belgium’s sovereign investment vehicle, La Société Fédérale de Participations et d’Investissement (“SFPI”), through an increase in FBB’s share capital in return for a payment of €4.7 billion; (2) the Netherlands would acquire a 49.9% stake in FBB’s Dutch subsidiary, FBN, for €4 billion; and (3) Luxembourg wouldnmake a €2.5 billion mandatorily convertible loan to FBB’s Luxembourg subsidiary, FBL, through conversion of which Luxembourg would hold a stake of 49.9% in FBL. At the same time, the BNB agreed to provide emergency liquidity assistance to FBB in the amount of €14.8 billion. The Boards of Fortis and FBB accepted the plans agreed by the governments. The capital increase by FBB in favour of Belgium was implemented on September 29, 2008. The Claimants say that their indirect interest in FBB was effectively halved from 4.81% to approximately 2.41%. Those transactions are described by the Parties as “the First Intervention.”". / At the same time, Belgium entered into negotiations with the French bank, BNP Paribas, with a view to selling a substantial stake in FBB. On October 6, 2008 Belgium issued a press release stating that it would take the remaining shares in FBB for €4.7 billion and that it would then transfer, back-to-back, 75% of the shares in FBB to BNP"
lawchange_text: This claim referred to mostly an ad hoc, emergencial decision by Belgium, in the context of the 2008 crisis. No specific policies or laws seem to have been changed because of this particular case. Fortis' "bad assets" were sold to an American group in 2013. Link: https://bloom.bg/2uk2zDd

dissent_description: One arbitrator dissented on the rulings on Fair and Equitable Treatment and on Effective Means for Exercising rights, defending these should have been upheld. Dissenting opinion is published on Spanish only. From IA Reporter: In his dissenting opinion (now available for download), Mr. Tawil departed from this view, and thought that legitimate expectations can arise even where there has been no specific commitment (for e.g. as in a contract with an investor). Thus, Mr. Tawil opined that legitimate expectations might derive from a broader legal framework in a situation where certain incentives or benefits are offered to a specific category of persons (rather than the general population). Of additional note, he took issue with the majority’s repeated comment that the recognition of legitimate expectations in a case such as this would impose an unwarranted “freezing” of a country’s laws. Mr. Tawil countered, that no such freezing need be effected; Rather, if such changes do occur, they would be accompanied by compensation to affected investors
law_text: From Award: "the Claimants claim that after attracting their investments in the photovoltaic generation sector, the Respondent has unlawfully amended the special regime regulating the industry, causing various losses./ 148. Royal Decree 1565/2010 was promulgated on 19 November 2010 and “Regulates and modifies certain aspects relating to the electricity production under the special regime” (“RD 1565/2010”).45 149. The Claimants base their claims on two specific aspects of RD 1565/2010, which will be described below, (a) elimination of regulated tariffs from the twenty-sixth year for solar photovoltaic facilities, and (b) introduction of a series of additional technical requirements promulgated on 19 November 2010 and “Regulates and modifies certain aspects relating to the electricity production under the special regime” (“RD 1565/2010”).\ 156. On 23 December 2010, Royal Decree-Law 14/2010 on “Establishment of urgent measures to amend the tariff deficit in the electricity sector” was promulgated (“RDL 14/2010”).  As indicated in its title, the objective of RDL 14/2010 is to amend the tariff deficit in the electricity sector and establish a set of measures “so that all stakeholders in the sector contribute an additional and shared effort to the reduction of the deficit in the electricity system”.
lawchange_text: From IEA (https://goo.gl/1uMLdX): This record is superseded by: Royal Decree 413/2014 on electricity generation by means of renewable, cogeneration and waste facilities. From IA Reporter: In at least five recent judgments, the Supreme Court has rejected administrative challenges lodged by energy investors against Royal Decree 413/2014 and Ministerial Order IET/1045/2014 – which, readers will recall, replaced the previous regulatory framework with a system where the “reasonable rate of return” for renewables investors hinged on the standard operating costs of an hypothetical “efficient” solar plant.\Royal Decree Law 14/2010 remains in force.

lawchange_text: "the Supreme Court of Canada has just overturned decades of precedent -- and implicitly the Eli Lilly ruling -- by making it easier for Big Pharma to gain patents on medicines that don't really work[6]:This reversal in AstraZeneca Canada Inc. v. Summary of the dispute: Claims arising out of a moratorium imposed by the Government of Ontario on offshore wind farms that indefinitely suspended claimant's investment project in this sector.

parties: Windstream Energy LLC v. The Government of Canada
invst_type: Ownership of WWIS (100 per cent shareholding), a Canadian corporation that had entered into a power purchase agreement under the Ontario Power Authority’s feed-in-tariff program to develop an offshore wind generation facility in Ontario.
invst_dispute: Claims arising out of a moratorium imposed by the Government of Ontario on offshore wind farms that indefinitely suspended claimant's investment project in this sector.
parties_drop: NA
law_text: From Award: "5. The dispute between the Parties arises out of an offshore wind electricity generation project in the Wolfe Island Shoals area in Ontario, Canada (the “Project” or the “WWIS Project”). The Project was undertaken following Ontario’s enactment of the Green Energy and Green Economy Act of 2009 (“GEGEA”) and the subsequent promulgation of additional rules and regulations, creating a Feed-in-Tariff (“FIT”) program (the “FIT Program”) for the development of renewable energy projects, including onshore and offshore wind. According to the Claimant, following the award of a Feed-in-Tariff Contract (the “FIT Contract”) to the Claimant, the Government of Ontario (also referred to as the “Government” or “Ontario”) delayed the approval of the required permits and authorizations, including those allowing access to Crown land, and eventually, on 11 February 2011, imposed a moratorium on the development of offshore wind that frustrated the Claimant’s attempts to develop the Project."
lawchange_text: The moratorium remains in force and the government has signaled that it will continue for many years: https://bit.ly/2KMWwBn
lawcount_text: The moratorium is currently being disputed by Tennant Energy (not on this dataset). Claim filed in 2017 and pending in UNCITRAL.
other_text: The moratorium persists, despite claims. The government alleges the need for more research before continuing with projects. Link from Nexis Uni: https://bit.ly/2MFFKBb

claimchoice_text: From Award (translation): "In its decision about jurisdiction, the Arbitral Tribunal decided to be competent only regarding the claims of granting fair and equitable treatment, referring to the taxation and canon process of the Regulatory Authority of Public Services. Consequently, the claims against the Republic of Costa Rica are based exclusively on an alleged violation of the standard of fair and equitable treatment as provisioned by Article 4 in the BIT. In the second phase of the arbitration, the claimants only refer to the taxation issue, giving up on its claim regarding the canon of Regulatory Authority of Public Services."
law_text: From Award (Translation): "259. The Claimants sustain that: (1) the taxation resolutions issued by the Regulatory Authority of Public Services between 2010 and 2015 were unreasonable and disproportional and were based on arbitrary and discretionary, (2) and thus are violations of Costa Rica's obligations under the BIT, and (3) given the aforementioned, the respondent should compensate the claimants for the costs.". Note: the claimants dispute a set of tax resolutions: ET 2010, ET 2011, ET 2012, ET 2013, ET 2014

claimchoice_text: From Award: "353. While  the  Tribunal  has  considered  each  of these  claims,  it  concludes  that  the  claim  invoking  Respondent’s  Article   10(1)  obligation  to  accord  investors fair  and  equitable treatment  provides  the  most  appropriate  legal  context  for  assessing  the  complex  factual  situation presented here.  The Tribunal’s decision in this regard fully resolves Claimants’ claim.   In  the  circumstances  here,  decision  of  the  remaining  claims  would  not  alter  the  outcome or affect the damages to which Claimants are entitled. 354.As other Tribunals have observed, considerations of economy – both jurisprudential and financial  – may  lead  a  tribunal  to conclude  that  it  need  not  address  issues  extraneous  to  those  essential  to  its  decision. Note: the tribunal justifies its reasoning on judicial economy by citing SGS Société Générale v. Paraguay and Micula v. Romania."
law_text: From Award: "144. without prior notice to CSP producers, Parliament adopted Law 15/2012, inter alia imposing a 7% tax on the total value of all energy fed into the  National  Grid  by  electricity  producers  (“TVPEE”)  and  eliminating  premiums  for  electricity generated with gas. \145. Royal  Decree  Law  2/2013  of  1  February  2013  (“RDL  2/2013”)  then  eliminated  the  Premium Option altogether, leaving CSP producers the option of either the market price or  the fixed rate tariff.  RDL 2/2013 also cancelled the mechanism for updating the feed-in tariffs in accordance with the Consumer Price Index, substituting a different index lower than the CPI. \ 146. A much more drastic change came on 12 July 2013, with the enactment of Royal Decree Law  9/2013  (“RDL  9/2013”). 116 This  legislation  amended  Article  34  of  the  1997  Electricity Law (which created the Special Regime for renewables producers) and repealed RD 661/2007.  It eliminated the entire regime of fixed tariffs and premiums, and substituted a system providing for “specific remuneration” based on “standard” (but not actual) costs per unit of installed power, plus standard amounts for operating costs.  However, many key details of the new replacement regime were left uncertain.\117 Then, in December 2013, Respondent  adopted  Law  24/2013,  which  superseded  the  1997  Electricity  Law  and  completely eliminated the distinction between the Ordinary and Special Regimes."
lawchange_text:
From IEA: Laws still in force: Law 15/012, RD 2/2013, RD 9/2013, Law 24/013, RD 413/2014 and IET 1045/2014. Modified: RD 661/2007, modified by RD 2/2013 in February 2013. Modification of the remuneration system of regulated activities and the remuneration formula of the special regime facilities, with the intent to avoid the increase of the tariff deficit and that consumers bear these costs through higher electric tolls. With the entry into force of RDL 2/2013, this premium is replaced by a regulated tariff.
Changes in the tariff to be received by certain facilities depending on the technology used. More details: https://goo.gl/AVWN2o
lawcount_text: Same law is disputed in ig15

law_text: Introduction of the solar levy: "On 14 December 2010, the Respondent adopted Act 402/2020 Coll., ("Act 402/2010"), which limited the contribution of consumers to the RES subsidies (FIT or green bonus) to CZK 370 /MWH. This created a shortfall between the funds raised from the final consumers and the amount of subsidy to be paid to RES producers. Act 402/2010 provided that this shortfall would be financed from the state budget. The state budget, consequently, needed additonal revenues to fund this expenditure. 49. These additional revenues were to come from multiple sources, one of which was a 26% so-called solar levy (the “Solar Levy”) which was introduced through Act 402/2010.29 The Solar Levy was to remain in force for a period of three years and applied to PV plants put into operation between 1 January 2009 and 31 December 2010 (...)"

dissent_description: One arbitrator does not agree with the majority view about the treatment of "legitimate expectations" that integrate the standard of "fair and equitable treatment" established by Article 10(1) of the ECT. His view is that the tribunal should have ruled on the previsibility of the changes in the regulatory framework.
claimchoice_text:
About not ruling on the breach to stable, equitable, favorable and transparent conditions for investment, from Award: 
"764. The tribunal does not find in this article an autonomous obligations for the respondent-State to foment and create stable and transparent conditions for investment in its territory whose violation per se would generate rights in favor of investors. It would be absurd, for instance, that an investor files a claim against a State for compensation because the State has not fomented and created stable and transparent conditions for investment in its territory, if such abstention were not the cause of violation for another obligation, such as conceding to the investment fair and equitable treatment, full protection and security, etc... 766. As such, the tribunal will not examine separately the supposed violation by the Kingdom of Spain of creating stable and transparent conditions for investment in its territory. / About not ruling on the umbrella clause: 767. The tribunal does not consider convenient to analyze separately the supposed violation by the Kingdom of Spain of Article 10(1) of the ECT mentioned by the claimant, since this petition is based on an interpretation of the last sentence on Article 10(1), which is not shared by the tribunal. The tribunal cites Noble Ventures, Inc v. Rumanía"
law_text: From Award: "332. With the adoption of RD 9/2013, in force since July 14th 2013, and the new regime for Specific Distribution, parameters were applied which were not accounted for by the investors in the moment they decided to invest. The Kingdom of Spain has never committed to an immutable legal framework, but it had the obligation of ensuring stable, transparent and predictibel legal and economic environments."
lawchange_text: From IEA, RD 9/2013 still in force.

invst_type: Interests in a photovoltaic energy generation project in Italy.

parties_drop: Blusun S.A., Jean-Pierre Lecorcier and Michael Stein v. Italian Republic
law_text: The investors claim that a series of measures taken by the Italian government deprived them from their investment. The measures are (some passages are exact extracts from the Award and others are adaptations): (1) the Legislative Decree 28/11 (the Romani Decree), which entered into force on 29 March 2011. The stated objective of the Romani Decree was to implement Directive 2009/28/EC on the promotion of the use energy from renewable sources, and to amend and repeal Directives 2001/77/EC and 2003/30/EC. Among other reforms, the Romani Decree established that the feed-in tariffs adopted by the Decree of 6 August 2010 (the Third Energy Account) applied only to plants that entered into operation before 31 May 2011 (and not, as originally established in the 6 August 2010 Decree, to plants that entered into operation by 31 December 2013). The Romani Decree also established that any solar plants to be built on agricultural land would be eligible for feed-in tariffs only if the plant had a capacity of less than 1 MW and occupied less than 10% of the parcel on which it was erected. This limitation, however, did not apply to those plants that entered into operation within one year of the entry into force of the Romani Decree (i.e., by 29 March 2012). (2) The Fourth Energy Account, adopted by Decree of 5 May 2011. The Claimants highlight that the Fourth Energy Account limited to specific amounts the incentives available for large plants and created an on-line register of plants eligible for feed-in tariffs administered by the GSE and that the plants' capacitiy had an impact on the amount of the feed-in tariffs; (3) The Law Decree 1 of 24 January 2012 provided that some solar plants built on agricultural land would no longer benefit from the incentives set out in the Romani Decree. It established some exceptions for that, for which the investor did not qualify, though. (4) a stop-work order, which claimants argued was the final blow to the project.
lawchange_text: According to the IEA website, there was no change to Legislative Decree 28/11. No evidence available for the Fourth Energy Account, for the Decree 1/2012 and for the stop-work order.  One general change from this arbitration (and others related to energy reforms) was Italy's withdrawal from the ECT: https://bit.ly/2usqob6
lawcount_text: This is the first case that disputes measures taken by the government that changed the regime of incentives to clean energy. Case ig146 disputes two measures disputed in this one: Law Decre 28/2011 and the Fourth Energy Account. Cases ig217, ig118, ig85, ig152, ig100 and ig201 are all related, but they are not specific with the regards the laws being disputed (documents not available).
other_text: The investor is seeking annulment proceedings, but no documents are available, so it is not possible to determine the content of the annulment.

parties_drop: Bear Creek Mining Corporation v. Republic of Peru
dissent_description: According to one of the arbitrators, "The circumstances in which Supreme Decree 032-2011 was adopted – in particular the failure to give the Claimant a right to be heard before its adoption – also gave rise, in my view, to a violation of Article 805, being a violation of the obligation to offer 'fair and equitable treatment'."
claimchoice_text: From the award: "The Tribunal has found above that Supreme Decree 032 constituted an indirect expropriation. Therefore, there is no need to examine whether it also constituted a direct expropriation. The Parties have not presented arguments related to the legal consequences of a finding of a direct expropriation, and such a finding indeed would not change or add to those that follow from an indirect expropriation" (paragraph 429). There are other, similar arguments throughout the award: "In view of the above conclusions, it is not necessary for the Tribunal to examine whether it was also issued in a non-discriminatory manner, because such a finding would not change or add to the legal consequences of the measure" (paragraph 448). "The Tribunal has found above that Supreme Decree 032 constituted an unlawful indirect expropriation. Therefore, there is no need to examine whether it also constituted a breach of a duty to afford Claimant fair and equitable treatment" (paragraph 533). The same applies to full protection and security and arbitrary/discriminatory measures.
law_text: According to Article 71 of the Peruvian Constitution, foreigners are not allowed to possess natural resources in border regions unless they can make a case to the Peruvian Government for "public necessity." Supreme Decree 083-2007 made such case and allowed the Claimant to acquire mining rights. However, after a wave of protests against the Claimant, Peru revoked the company's mining rights (Supreme Decree 032-2011)

For the reasons set forth above, the Tribunal decides as follows: (1) The Tribunal decides that the Claimant not having satisfied the conditions required under DR-CAFTA Article 18.10.1, its request for arbitration was time-barred and the present Tribunal has no jursidiction over the claims."
law_text: From Award: "On August 18, 2010, the Environmental Ministry, through Communication No. DEA-3867-10, informed Corona that the Joama Project was "not environmentally viable" (the "Negative Environmental Decision" or the Communication No. DEA-3867-10). The Negative Environmental Decision was taken by Resolution 737-10 of the Environmental Ministry's Technical Evaluation Committee ("TEC") following a meeting of July 28, 2010 ("Resolution 737-10"). According to the Claimant, the Negative Environmental Decision failed to proffer actual reasons, was only two pages long, did not provide explanations, nor set out any procedure by which the decision could be appealed or could be submitted to reconsideration. The Claimant further submits that Resolution 737-10 was never served on the Claimant. The Respondent, on the other hand, argues that the communication not only informed Walvis of the Ministry's decision to reject the license application, but also of the reasons why such decision was made, as it indicated that the rejection was based on Articles 118, 120, 126, 129, and 8 of Law No. 64-00 on the Environment and Natural Resources."
lawchange_text: Licenses were not granted to the company, and there were not any changes to Law 64.00
lawcount_text: Investors are disputing the government's denial in issuing environmental permits, which are regulated by Law 64.00. The same happens in ig135.

invst_dispute: Claims arising out of the alleged Government's failure to provide proper vigilance and governmental regulatory supervision over the national financial system, which led the 137 individual claimants to lose their deposits made in a Costa Rican business acting under a Ponzi scheme.
source1: For reinvestment data, see Luke Eric Peterson. "Class-action Claims Against Costa Rica Can't Clear Jurisdictional Hurdle." IA Reporter, 26 May 2010. Accessed online via https://www.iareporter.com/articles/class-action-claim-against-costa-rica-cant-clear-jurisdictional-hurdle/.
parties_drop: Alasdair Ross Anderson and others v. Republic of Costa Rica
claimchoice_text:
"the   Respondent’s   preliminary   objection   ratione   materiae   to   the   Tribunal’s   
jurisdiction  must  be  accepted  on  grounds  that  the  deposits  made  by  the  Claimants  
with the Villalobos brothers did not constitute an “investment” as that term is defined 
in Article I of the Canada-Costa Rica BIT; the Tribunal is accordingly without jurisdiction to entertain the dispute submitted to it either in part or in whole"
other_text: Another case regarding the Villalobos brothers' ponzi scheme
